coredata.dc:title: Homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of chloramphenicol and amantadine via the duplex FRET between carbon dots and WS2 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Baolei
Li, Hongfang
Sun, Jiefang
Mujtaba Mari, Ghulam
Ai, Jun
Han, Diangang
Yu, Xuezhi
Wen, Kai
Shen, Jianzhong
Wang, Zhanhui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we proposed a duplex and homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of amantadine (AMD) and chloramphenicol (CAP) residue in chicken breast with both high sensitivity and short assay time. The immunoassay was based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between hapten-labeled carbon dots (CDs) and antibody-modified WS2 nanosheets. To achieve the duplex FRET, polyethyleneimine-functionalized blue and green emissive CDs with separated emission were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method and directly coupled with the haptens of AMD and CAP, serving as the energy donors. The antibodies were modified on the surface of WS2 nanosheets with high quenching efficiency to construct the energy acceptor. The specific immunoreaction could trigger the efficient FRET between the donors and the acceptors, causing the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The developed immunoassay was applied to simultaneously detect AMD and CAP, having the detection limit of 0.10 ng g−1 and 0.06 ng g−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Homogeneous immunoassay
Carbon dots
WS2 nanosheets
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Chloramphenicol
Amantadine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amine functionalized CDs and hapten-labeled CDs
Simultaneous multiplex detection of AMD and CAP in chicken
Synthesis and characterization of Ab-modified WS2 NSs
Principle and mechanism of FRET immunoassay
Optimization of detection conditions and method development
Analytical performance of the FRET immunoassay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the b-CDs and g-CDs: (a) 2D fluorescence plot of the b-CDs, Exmax = 355 nm and Emmax = 442 nm, (right inset: b-CDs under UV light). (b) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the b-CDs. (c) AFM images of the of the b-CDs. (d) 2D fluorescence plot of the g-CDs, Exmax = 400 nm and Emmax = 520 nm, (right inset: g-CDs under UV light). (e) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the of the g-CDs. (f) AFM images of the of the g-CDs.

(a) Full XPS spectra of the b-CDs. (b) Full XPS spectra of the g-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of the b-CDs and g-CDs. (d) Fluorescence decay spectra for the b-CDs and g-CDs. The lifetimes of the b-CDs and g-CDs were 7.5 ns and 9.0 ns, respectively. (e) Illustration of the tunable PL of b-CDs and g-CDs. (HOMO: highest occupied molecular orbital, LUMO: lowest unoccupied molecular orbital).

Characterization of WS2 NSs and Ab-modified WS2 NSs. (a) TEM and EDS images of WS2 NSs. (b) AFM images of WS2 NSs. (c) DLS analysis of the size distribution (solid lines) and zeta-potential values (dashed lines) of bare WS2 NSs and Ab-modified WS2 NSs.

(a) Spectra overlapping between the absorption spectra of WS2 NSs and the emission spectra of b-CDs and g-CDs. (b) The fluorescence quenching of hapten-labeled CDs versus a series of Ab-labeled WS2 NSs. The concentrations of hapten-labeled b-CDs and g-CDs were 10 μg mL−1 and 20 μg mL−1, respectively. (c) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of AMD and CAP. Linear regression analysis of the AMD and CAP detection: ΔF = 48.4 + 284.9 C (R2 = 0.981); ΔF = 82.1 + 422.0 C (R2 = 0.989), respectively. (d) Specificity of the FRET-based immunoassay against AMD, CAP, ribavirin, moroxydine, acyclovir, enrofloxacin, sulfamethazine, ceftiofur. The spiked concentrations of AMD, CAP and the other drugs were 2.0 ng mL−1; vertical bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 6). (e) Recovery and precision of the fluorescent immunoassay in spiked samples, including intra-assay (A, n = 6) and inter-assay (B, n = 6). (f) Correlation between results from ELISA and FRET-based immunoassay for analyzing AMD-spiked samples (0.25, 1.0, 3.0 ng g−1) and CAP-spiked samples (0.2, 0.5, 1.0 ng g−1); R2 = 0.975 for AMD detection and R2 = 0.979 for CAP detection.
(a) synthesis of Ab-modified WS2 NSs. (b) schematic illustration of FRET-based duplex immunoassay for AMD and CAP detection.
An overview and comparison of the recently immunoassays for the detection of AMD and CAP residues.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: HRP refers to the horseradish peroxidase. bcut-off value is defined as the minimum analyte to produce colorless test line in a LFIA. cSPR is surface plasmon resonance. dSPR-based immunoassay and interferometric immunoassay are label-free detection strategies based on the antibody-antigen interaction. e2′,6′-DiMethylcarbonylphenyl-10-sulfopropylacridinium-9-carboxylate 4′-NHS Ester.

head.ce:title: Homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of chloramphenicol and amantadine via the duplex FRET between carbon dots and WS2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health
Beijing Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Diagnostic and Traceability for Food Poisoning, Beijing Research Center for Preventive Medicine, Beijing 100013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Center of Kunming Customs, Kunming 650299, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Center of Kunming Customs, Kunming 650299, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we proposed a duplex and homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of amantadine (AMD) and chloramphenicol (CAP) residue in chicken breast with both high sensitivity and short assay time. The immunoassay was based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between hapten-labeled carbon dots (CDs) and antibody-modified WS2 nanosheets. To achieve the duplex FRET, polyethyleneimine-functionalized blue and green emissive CDs with separated emission were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method and directly coupled with the haptens of AMD and CAP, serving as the energy donors. The antibodies were modified on the surface of WS2 nanosheets with high quenching efficiency to construct the energy acceptor. The specific immunoreaction could trigger the efficient FRET between the donors and the acceptors, causing the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The developed immunoassay was applied to simultaneously detect AMD and CAP, having the detection limit of 0.10 ng g−1 and 0.06 ng g−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is a worldwide concern to sustainable consumption, which is threat by numerous chemical hazards during the production and processing. For example, the residues of antibiotics and other hazardous drugs in animal-derived foods could pose serious threats to human health (Nerín, Aznar, & Carrizo, 2016). The drug residues could directly cause the damages after they entered and accumulated in human body, leading to poisoning, allergy, etc. Moreover, the accumulation of the drug residues may cause dysbacteriosis in guts and induce the emergence of serious drug-resistant strains (Lammie & Hughes, 2016). Thus, antibiotics and antivirals like chloramphenicol (CAP) and amantadine (AMD) with significant side effects and toxicities are banned from use in livestock by national authorities. (Davis et al., 2009; Regulation No. 560. Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of the People’s Republic of China, 2005, 2005). At present, the confirmative detection of drug residuals in food mainly relies on the instrumental analysis such as liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry with high detectability and accuracy (Masiá, Suarez-Varela, Llopis-Gonzalez, & Picó, 2016). However, these methods have the disadvantages of expensive equipment, sophisticated operations and time-consuming sample pretreatments, which are not suitable for on-site analysis of large numbers of samples. Therefore, it is highly demanded to develop a cheap, simple, and rapid method with highly sensitive and accuracy for the on-site screening of food samples. Immunoassays based on the specific molecular recognition between the antigen and antibody are alternative tools for monitoring the chemical contaminants in foodstuff (Li et al., 2017). The outstanding properties of immunoassays like high-sensitivity, low-cost and simple-operation make them to be the efficient strategy to achieve the rapid screening purpose. Considering the original intention of a screening method, a homogeneous immunoassay based on fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) with simple and separation-free operation is a practical and promising format. FRET strategy usually involves a non-radiative transfer process of energy from a fluorophore serving as a donor to an energy acceptor via dipole–dipole interaction. Recently, FRET-based immunoassays are increasingly used in food analysis (Liu et al., 2018). In a typical FRET-based immunoassay, organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are the most commonly used fluorescent probes, acting as the energy donors. However, the dyes usually suffer from the photobleaching; QDs need complicated and multistep procedures for synthesis and phase transformation from organic solvent to water.

As a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) refer to the fluorescent carbon nanocrystals of less than 10 nm in size. CDs usually have the advantages of simple synthesis, good fluorescence stability and low toxicity comparing with the dyes or QDs (Havrdova et al., 2016). Moreover, the fluorescence performance of the CDs can be easily regulated by controlling the reaction precursors and reaction conditions during the synthesis process (Miao et al., 2018). These superior properties make carbon dots to be the promising and “green” fluorescent probes in the construction of FRET-based immunoassay (Bu, Zhuang, Yang, & Ping, 2014; Hamd-Ghadareh, Salimi, Fathi, & Bahrami, 2017). However, most of the current FRET-based homogeneous immunoassays are for the single-target detection due to the difficulty in the select and construction of energy donor-accepter pairs. Considering the coexistence of multiple drug residues in food, it is necessary to establish a duplex and even multi-target immunoassay for effective food analysis. Recently, multiplex enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) have been developed for the detection of antibiotic residues. For instance, our lab has established a sensitive duplex chemiluminescent ELISA for the simultaneous detection of norfloxacin and sulfamethazine in milk (Yu et al., 2018); Han et al developed an octuplex lateral flow immunoassay for rapid detection of six kind of antibiotic residues in milk (Han et al., 2019). Compared with the heterogeneous strategies like ELISA and LFIA, FRET-based homogeneous immunoassay has advantages in detection speed and facile operation with no need of separation or washing steps.

In this research, we developed a duplex and homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) and amantadine (AMD) in skinless chicken breast. We synthesized PEI-functionalized blue and green emissive CDs, which had abundant amino groups as well as the separated and independent fluorescence emission peaks. We coupled the haptens of AMD and CAP to the CDs to construct two stable and eco-friendly fluorescent immune-probes, serving as the energy donors for the FRET system. Then, two-dimensional WS2 nanosheets (NSs) which had a broad absorption spectrum in the ultraviolet-visible band were synthesized. The sensitive and specific antibodies (Ab) of CAP and AMD were modified on the surface of WS2 NSs to construct the energy acceptor. The hapten-functionalized CDs could be adsorbed to the surface of WS2 NSs by the specific antigen-antibody binding. The efficient FRET would occur between the hapten-labeled CDs and the WS2 NSs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. Rapid and highly sensitive detection of CAP and AMD is realized by using the immunoassay via the competitive immunoreaction. The whole detection process didn’t need the time-consuming separation and washing steps and could be accomplished in a short time.

Citric acid, caffeic acid, ammonium tungstate hydrate ((NH4)10W12O41·2H2O,) and thiourea (CS(NH2)2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), trichloroacetic acid and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). Other common reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Chloramphenicol (CAP), amantadine (AMD), polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW = 3000), polyethyleneimine branched (PEI, MW = 800), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopro-pyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sigma Chemical Company (St. Louis, MO, USA). Amantadine succinate (AMD-HS, Fig. S1) and anti-AMD antibody (Ab) were prepared in our previous research (Wang et al., 2018). Chloramphenicol succinate (CAP-HS, Fig. S1) was obtained from Toronto Research Chemicals (Toronto, Canada). Anti-CAP Ab was also prepared in our previous research (Tao et al., 2014). Nunc 96-well microtitre plates were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Roskilde, Denmark). Dialysis bag (retain molecular weight 3500 Da) were purchased from Solarbio Science (Beijing, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. The detail buffers used in this study were described in Supporting Information.
Details regarding the apparatus and characterization are provided in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amine functionalized CDs and hapten-labeled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: PEI-functionalized blue emissive CDs (b-CDs) and green emissive CDs (g-CDs) were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method (Chung, Kim, Lee, & Park, 2017; Dong et al., 2019). Briefly, preparation of b-CDs: 0.5 g citric acid and 0.3 g PEI were dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water and stirred to form a clear solution. The solution was then transferred into a 20 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. The preparation of g-CDs was similar to that of b-CDs: 0.5 g caffeic acid and 0.3 g PEI were dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water and stirred to form a clear solution. The solution was then transferred into the poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. All prepared CDs were filtered through 0.22 μm membranes to remove the large dots and dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for at least 24 h to obtain the CDs solution. The CDs solutions were freeze-dried in vacuum dryer to get the solid powder.

Hapten-labeled CDs were prepared via the active-ester method. Two haptens (CAP-HS, AMD-HS) with carboxylic acid groups were activated and coupled to the amino groups on the surface of the PEI-functionalized CDs. Briefly, 1.0 mg mL−1 CAP-HS (or AMD-HS) was activated by adding an aqueous mixture of 3.0 mg mL−1 EDC and 6.0 mg mL−1 NHS with gently shaking for 2 h at RT. Then the mixture was dropped into the CDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1) and followed by incubation for 4 h to covalently immobilize the hapten onto the surface of CDs. The solution of the labeled CDs was dialyzed against ultrapure water for at least 24 h to remove the free hapten and was then re-dispersed in PBS buffer containing 0.05% PEG until used.

The WS2 nanosheets in this study were prepared via the hydrothermal synthesis (Liu et al., 2015; Piao et al., 2018). Briefly, 0.5 mmol ((NH4)10W12O41·2H2O,) and 30 mmol CS(NH2)2 and 0.25 mmol SDBS were dissolved in 35 mL distilled water under vigorous stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution. The pH value of the mixture was adjusted to seven by adding HCl solution. The solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was treated at 220 °C for 24 h to obtain black products, and naturally cooled down to room temperature. The as-prepared samples were washed several times with ethanol and distilled water and dried at 80 °C for 10 h under vacuum.

The Ab-functionalized WS2 NSs was prepared through the electrostatic adsorption between the WS2 NSs and the antibody (Li et al., 2019). Briefly, 10 μL anti-AMD Ab/anti-CAP Ab (1.0 mg mL−1) was added to 1.5 mL of WS2 NSs aqueous solution (100 μg mL−1). The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h and then blocked by adding 20 μL of 5% BSA at 37 °C for further 1 h. Subsequently, the above solution was centrifugated at 4000 g for 15 min at 4 °C and the supernatant was abandoned. The collected Ab-modified WS2 NSs were then re-dispersed in PBS buffer containing 0.1% BSA and was stored at 4° C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous multiplex detection of AMD and CAP in chicken
ce:section.ce:para: The negative skinless chicken breast samples (confirmed by HPLC) were provided by the National Reference Laboratory for Veterinary Drug Residues (Beijing, China). Pretreatment of sample: one grams of chicken breast were homogenized and put into a tube. Then, 0.5 mL of 10% trichloroacetic acid and 5 mL of ethyl acetate were added, and the mixture was vortexed vigorously for 10 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 4000g for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. One milliliter of the supernatant was evaporated to dryness under nitrogen flow, then was dissolved in 200 μL of PBS buffer (Xie et al., 2019). Samples spiked with AMD and CAP standards (0.2, 1.0, and 3.0 ng g−1; 0.2, 1.0, and 2.0 ng g−1, respectively) were extracted with the above treatment procedures before analysis.

The fluorescence immunoassay was performed as follows: 50 μL WS2 NS and 100 μL sample solution was mixed in the microplate, then 50 μL of AMD-labeled b-CDs (10 μg mL−1) and CAP-labeled g-CDs (20 μg mL−1) solution was added. Fluorescence intensity of CDs (Ex = 355 nm/Em = 446 nm for AMD detection, Ex = 400 nm/Em = 525 nm for CAP detection) were measured.

In this study, we used CDs as fluorescent energy donors in the FRET immunoassay system. To construct the dual-wavelength FRET immunoassay, we synthesized the b-CDs and g-CDs with independent emission wavelengths. The amino-functionalized b-CDs and g-CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method, in which citric acid and caffeic acid is used as the carbon source, respectively; PEI is used as a nitrogen source and surface functionalization reagent. Then, the haptens of AMD and CAP were coupled with the blue and green fluorescent CDs, respectively, to prepare two fluorescent probes which served as energy donors for the FRET sensing system.

The 2D fluorescence plot of b-CDs and g-CDs showed the maximum emission (Emmax) wavelength of 442 nm, 520 nm and maximum excitation (Exmax) wavelength of 355 nm, 400 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 ). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of b-CDs and g-CDs were presented in Fig. 1b and e. The results showed that the two CDs were well-monodispersed, with similar particle sizes of 3.0 nm to 5.0 nm. The high-resolution (HR) TEM images indicate that both of the two CDs had the lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1b and e, Fig. S2), corresponding to the (100) lattice spacing of graphene. We also used the atomic force microscope (AFM) to evaluate the monodispersity of the two CDs showing the uniform height distribution with the average heights of approximate 3.0 nm. Both TEM and AFM results confirmed the good monodispersity of the prepared CDs in this study. The UV–vis absorption of the two CDs displaying the region of 230 nm–550 nm was showed in Fig. S3. The absorption peaks observed at 250 nm–280 nm are corresponding to the π–π* transitions of CC and CN bonds in the aromatic rings for both the b-CDs and g-CDs (Fig. S3). The peaks around 360 nm and 400 nm correlate with the n–π* electronic transition at the surface of the b-CDs and g-CDs, respectively. Previous studies have shown that the fluorescence properties of CDs are attributed to the intrinsic carbon core as well as the surface states (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2015; Wang et al., 2014). The surface states of the CDs are the main factors which regulate the photoluminescence (PL) properties. In this study, we regulated the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs by using different carbon sources (citric acid or caffeic acid) in the hydrothermal synthesis process to obtain the emission-independent b-CDs and g-CDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were used to study the chemical composition of the b-CDs and g-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, both the XPS spectra of b-CDs and g-CDs had three main peaks: C 1s (including CC, CO/CN and CO), N 1s (pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N) and O 1s (CO and CO). The results of XPS confirmed that the b-CDs and g-CDs were successfully N-doped. According to the high-resolution XPS spectra of the two CDs, the CO bonds at ~288.8 eV and ~533.6 eV increased from b-CDs to g-CDs, implying the increased oxidation degree of the CDs (Fig. S4, Tables S1 and S2). The oxygen content increases from 9.32 to 13.48% for b-CDs to g-CDs, indicating the enhancement in the oxidation degree. The FTIR spectra of CDs were measured and displayed in Fig. 2c. It revealed that the two CDs samples possessed abundant groups such as OH (~3500 cm−1), NH (~3200 cm−1), CH (~2800 cm−1), CO (~1750 cm−1), CN (~1350 cm−1), CO (~1060 cm−1). An enhancement of the characteristic stretching vibration of CO bonds at 1750 cm−1 was found comparing b-CDs with g-CDs, indicating an increase of the oxidation degree and consistent with the XPS results. The above XPS and FTIR results exhibit that the increase of oxidation degree of g-CDs leads to the green fluorescence, comparing with the b-CDs. The quantum yields of the b-CDs and g-CDs were 64.3% and 36.2%, respectively (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of the two CDs were measured. The g-CDs exhibited longer fluorescence lifetime than b-CDs (Fig. 2d, Table S3), confirming that blue and green emissions were attributed to different individual emitting states. As shown in Fig. 2e, possible model responsible for the related energy-state structures and fluorescence emissions were illustrated (Chen et al., 2017). Abundant O-related state (π*) like CO were formed during the solvothermal synthesis of g-CDs using caffeic acid as the precursor. The emerged O-related state of g-CDs could non-radiatively relax to the defect state to lead to the long-wavelength fluorescence emission (Hu, Trinchi, Atkin, & Cole, 2015). The emission of b-CDs was caused by the transition of defect state (from π to π*, HOMO to LUMO), while the red-shifted emission of g-CDs was attributed to the transitions from π state to O-related state (π*) on the surfaces of the CDs (HOMO to LUMO). In summary, the oxidation degree of CDs could result in the tunable PL properties.

The b-CDs and g-CDs were modified by the haptens of AMD-HS and CAP-HS through the EDC/NHS coupling reaction, respectively (Scheme 1 ) (Sidhu, Singh, Garg, & Singh, 2017). Then the obtained probes were purified by dialysis against water to remove the unreacted chemicals and freeze-dried for use. We used the silica gel thin layer chromatography (TLC) to character the modified CDs. The displayed chromatogram of the TLC in Fig. S6 showed that both of the two modified CDs had different retention factor (Rf) values comparing with the naked CDs. The results were due to the reduced polarity of the CDs after been modified by the haptens. The haptens especially the AMD-HS had lower molecular polarity than the free amino group on the surfaces of the CDs. The results indicated the successful synthesis of the two hapten-labeled CDs. The hapten-labeled CDs showed slight changes in fluorescence emission spectra as well as the good fluorescence stability. The Emmax of b-CDs and g-CDs shifted from 442 nm to 446 nm and 520 nm to 525 nm after been labeled by AMD-HS and CAP-HS, respectively (Fig. S7). The fluorescence stability of the hapten-labeled CDs within 7 d were evaluated. The result in Fig. S8 showed that both of the two CDs could remain at least 90% of the original fluorescence intensity after the storage of 7 d. Therefore, the CDs showed the advantages of simple synthesis, low-cost, eco-friendly and good stability when used as the fluorescence energy donor in a FRET system compared with the conventional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Ab-modified WS2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, two-dimensional metal dichalcogenides like tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs) showed great potential in bioassay because of the outstanding properties like large surface area, easy-modification, broad absorption spectrum and good quenching ability (Barua, Dutta, Gogoi, Devi, & Khan, 2017; Zuo et al., 2016). In this study, the WS2 NSs was applied as the fluorescence quencher in the FRET-based immunoassay. We synthesized the WS2 NSs via the bottom-up hydrothermal strategy. Ammonium tungstate was the wolfram source, and thiourea acted as the sulfur source as well as the reducing agent. The typical morphologies of the WS2 NSs were characterized and observed by TEM and the AFM. The TEM image of the WS2 NSs in Fig. 3 a indicated the graphene-like morphologies with slight corrugations. A lattice spacing of 0.28 nm was observed in the high-resolution TEM image, which was corresponding to the (100) plane of the WS2 NSs (Sang et al., 2015). The elemental EDS showed two main peaks for tungsten and sulfur, respectively. We also conducted the AFM test to study the nanoscale structures of the WS2 NSs. The result of AFM testing in Fig. 3b showed that the thickness of the WS2 NSs was about 5 nm, and no obvious agglomeration was found among the neighboring nanosheets. The SEM image displayed in Fig. S9a revealed the flaky structure of the WS2 NSs, which matched the results of TEM and AFM. The solution of WS2 NSs showed the Tyndall effect owing to the high hydrophily and good dispensation in water (Fig. S9b). The obtained Raman spectra of the WS2 NSs was displayed in Fig. S10a. There were two feature peaks at wavelength near 355 cm−1 and 425 cm−1, corresponding to E2g and A1g modes of the WS2 NSs, respectively. The crystalline structure and phase composition of the WS2 NSs were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The obtained XRD pattern of the WS2 NSs is shown in Fig. S10b. The characteristic diffraction peaks like (0 0 2) peak at 14.4°, (101) peak at 32.4° and (110) peak at 57.2° were observed. All the peaks could be in good agreement with the hexagonal WS2 phase, which is consistent with the standard JCPDS card no. 08–0237 (Zhang et al., 2016). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also employed for the chemical analysis of the WS2 NSs (Fig. S11a). The XPS spectra exhibited main signal peaks for C, W, S, N and O elements. As shown in the fine XPS spectra (Fig. S11b and c), three main characteristic peaks of W were located at 32.1 eV, 34.5 eV and 37.1 eV for W 4f7/2, W 4f5/2 and W 5p3/2 respectively. Another two main peaks of S were at 161.7 eV and 163.1 eV, which were corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively (Liu et al., 2015). All of the above results showed the successful preparation of the WS2 NSs.

The antibody was immobilized on the surface of the WS2 NSs via electrostatic interaction and stacking (Li et al., 2018). The WS2 NSs have the lateral structure of large surface area, which is conducive to loading the antibody. Then, the extra sites on the WS2 surface were blocked by BSA. The surface modification of WS2 NSs was validated by dynamic light scattering (DLS). According to the DLS data in Fig. 3c, the average zeta potential of the WS2 NSs increased from −30.6 mV to −21.4 mV after the surface modification. This increase was attributed to the electrostatically attaching of protein onto the surface of the WS2 NSs. Moreover, the average hydrodynamic size of the WS2 NSs increased from 1012 nm to 1089 nm after been incubated with Ab and BSA (Fig. 3c). Both the changes of the hydrodynamic size and the zeta potential of the WS2 NSs indicated that the Ab were successfully immobilized on the surface of the WS2 NSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of FRET immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is the most frequently used quencher in FRET-based immunoassays owing to their facile synthesis and larger extinction coefficient (Peltomaa et al., 2018). However, the absorption spectra of AuNPs is near 520 nm which is relatively narrow to overlap with the emission spectra of two CDs for developing the duplex FRET-based immunoassay. Thus, the WS2 NSs with broad absorption spectra was used as an effective fluorescence quencher (Zuo et al., 2016).

The sensing mechanism for the duplex drug detection was shown in Scheme 1. The developed FRET immunoassay mainly consisted of fluorescence donors and acceptors. The two b-CDs and g-CDs functionalized by AMD-HS and CAP-HS acted as the FRET donors and the Ab-modified WS2 NSs acted as the FRET acceptor, respectively. The immobilized antibody on the WS2 NSs could recognize and bind to the specific antigen, and subsequently contributes to the absorption of CDs onto WS2 NSs, causing the efficient FRET. The independent emission of hapten-labeled b-CDs (Emmax at 446 nm) and g-CDs (Emmax at 525 nm) could avoid the interfering effects in the fluorescent signal output, suggesting that the two CDs were suitable bi-color FRET nanoprobes with single excitation. To achieve the simultaneously detection of the two drug targets, WS2 NSs were employed as the efficient quencher and FRET receptor. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, the board UV–vis absorption of WS2 NSs displayed a board optical absorption from 300 nm to 700 nm, which could overlap with the fluorescence emission of the two CDs. This spectrum overlapping between the two CDs and the WS2 NSs ensured the feasibility of the duplex FRET immunoassay (Zuo et al., 2016). The hapten-labeled CDs would be captured on the surface of WS2 NSs through the antibody-antigen immune recognition in the absence of the drug targets. The absorption of CDs onto WS2 NSs would cause fluorescence quenching of CDs via the occurring FRET. The antibodies would competitively bind to the target molecules (AMD or CAP) if there existed in the sample solution, resulting in the release of CDs from the WS2 NSs as well as the fluorescence recovery (Scheme 1b). Thus, the developed fluorescent immunoassay could achieve the simultaneous determination of two drug residual independently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions and method development
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previously reports, the WS2 NSs usually have high fluorescence quenching ability (Xi et al., 2014). Prior to the construction of immunoassay, the ratio between hapten-labeled CDs and Ab-labeled WS2 NSs were optimized for avoiding the non-specific quenching caused by inner filter effect or the physical adsorption, and achieving maximum quenching efficiency. As established in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased with the increasing concentrations of WS2 NSs from 5 µg mL−1 to 100 µg mL−1. The FRET-based fluorescence quenching was caused by the adsorption of hapten-labelled CDs on Ab-modified WS2 NSs, which was triggered by the specific antigen–antibody recognition and binding (Scheme 1b). The quenching efficiency was calculated as E = (F0 − Fq)/F0, where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs with BSA-WS2 NSs, and Fq was the fluorescence intensity of CDs after quenched by Ab-modified WS2 NSs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency reached 76.3% and 81.6% when the concentrations of WS2 NSs was 30 µg mL−1. The quenched fluorescence of CDs could be restored when the target molecules (AMD and CAP) were present (Fig. S12). Thus, 30 µg mL−1 of WS2 NSs was chosen as the working concentrations for the construction of the FRET-immunoassay. Moreover, the different immunoreaction time (0.5–20 min, Fig. S13a) and pH variation of buffer (5.2–9.6, Fig. S13b) were studied to optimize the main detection conditions. We also used the quenching efficiency to select the optimal conditions. The quenching efficiency of the two hapten-labeled CDs achieved maximum after the incubation of 10 min at pH of 7.4. Based on the results, pH = 7.4 and incubation time of 10 min were selected for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the FRET immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the calibration curves for AMD and CAP detection were developed in the re-dissolved extract of blank chicken breast. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs-WS2 NSs system in the presence of a series of AMD and CAP standard were measured and displayed in Fig. 4c. The fluorescence intensities were restored with the increased concentration of analytes. Good linearity between the ΔF intensity and the concentration of analytes were obtained. ΔF = F − F0, where F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensity of homogeneous FRET-based immunoassay in the presence and absence of analytes, C was the concentration of the analytes. The limit of detection (LOD) were 0.10 ng g−1 and 0.06 ng g−1 for AMD and CAP estimated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, respectively. The specificity of the FRET-based immunoassay was revealed using several other common antibiotics and antivirotics, including ribavirin, moroxydine, acyclovir, enrofloxacin, sulfamethazine, ceftiofur. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, only AMD and CAP led to enhanced fluorescence intensity instead of other drugs. These results indicate that the immunoassay possessed high specificity for the simultaneous detecting AMD and CAP. The recovery tests of the spiked samples including intra-assay and inter-assay were conducted to estimate the analytical reproducibility (Fig. 4e). The matrix effect of the chicken breast could influence the quantification detection of AMD and CAP. The extraction by organic solvents are the most commonly used to eliminate the matrix interference. However, the organic solvents in the obtained extract would interfere the antibody-antigen interaction as well as the sensitivity of the immunoassays. Diluting of the extract is a frequently used approach to reduce the effect of organic solvents on the immunoassay (Dong et al., 2019). However, the dilution approach usually sacrifices the sensitivity of the method because it reduces the concentration of the target in the extract. Therefore, the matrix effect of the chicken was eliminated through the “extraction–evaporation–redissolving” strategy (Xie et al., 2019). Blank chicken breast was spiked with different concentrations of AMD and CAP, and the recoveries were measured. Results in Fig. 4e showed that the average recoveries of intra-assay (inter-assay) were in the range of 78.8–93.0% (81.5–90.2%) and 83.1–91.7% (76.4–91.2%) for AMD and CAP, respectively. The intra-assay and inter-assay coefficient of variations for AMD and CAP detection ranged from 6.8% to 12.7%. In order to evaluate the suitability, the spiked samples were simultaneously analyzed by the FRET-based immunoassay and ELISA method using the same immunoreagents (Fig. S14). The good correlations between the FRET-immunoassay and the ELISA results (R2 = 0.971 for AMD and R2 = 0.979 for CAP) confirmed the reliability of the developed immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of AMD and CAP (Fig. 4f).

Recently, various immunoassays for detection of AMD and CAP have been developed, and the current FRET-based immunoassay was compared with other reported strategies (Table 1 ). Most of the previously researches focused on the detection of signal target, while simultaneous detection of AMD and CAP was achieved in our study. The FRET-based and separation-free homogenous immunoassay also took shorter assay time than heterogeneous one. Moreover, the CDs-based signal probe exhibited superior properties of low cost, facile synthesis and low toxicity compared with the conventional fluorescent signal indicator like QDs and fluorescent nanosphere. In a word, the FRET-based immunoassay developed in this study provided a simple, sensitive, accuracy and rapid tool for monitoring multiple prohibited drugs in chicken.

In this work, a duplex and homogeneous FRET-based immunoassay using CDs and WS2 NSs was developed for the simultaneous and sensitive analysis of AMD and CAP residues in chicken breast. Blue and green-emissive CDs was successfully synthesized in one pot, resulting from different degrees of oxidation of the CDs. Two independent energy donors were successfully constructed by coupling haptens to the CDs. Efficient FRET-based quenching could occur between the hapten-labeled CDs and the Ab-modified WS2 NSs. Sensitive and specific detection of AMD and CAP was achieved via the competitive immunoreaction-induced fluorescence quenching. Overall, the established immunoassay was appropriate for the rapid screening of drug residues in food. The proposed approach of our study possessed two features: (a) the facile FRET-based immunoassay provided a promising strategy for the detection of drug residues without separation and washing steps. (b) compared with organic dyes and QDs, CDs exhibit superior properties like facile synthesis, cost-effective, low toxicity and good photostability when used as the fluorescent signal probe. Furthermore, this research also extended the application of CDs in food analysis and immunoassays.

Baolei Dong: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Hongfang Li: Writing - original draft, Data curation. Jiefang Sun: Software, Methodology. Ghulam Mujtaba Mari: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jun Ai: Resources. Diangang Han: Resources. Xuezhi Yu: Validation, Supervision. Kai Wen: Validation, Supervision. Jianzhong Shen: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Zhanhui Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Chinese Ministry of Science and Technology for the National Key Research and Development Program of China (NO. 2017YFE0110800) and the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program (No. 727864).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cut-off on demand: Adjustment of the threshold level of an immunochromatographic assay for chloramphenicol
Analytical Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zvereva, E., Byzova, N., Sveshnikov, P., Zherdev, A., & Dzantiev, B., (2015). Cut-off on demand: adjustment of the threshold level of an immunochromatographic assay for chloramphenicol. Analytical Methods, 7, 6378–6384. Doi: 10.1039/c5ay00835b
